'''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004年}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007年}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: * Diameter: approximately 60 meters * Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: * Length: approximately 54 meters * Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). '''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004年}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007年}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: * Diameter: approximately 60 meters * Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: * Length: approximately 54 meters * Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004年}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007年}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: * Diameter: approximately 60 meters * Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: * Length: approximately 54 meters * Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. '''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004年}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007年}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: * Diameter: approximately 60 meters * Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: * Length: approximately 54 meters * Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). revision.text:
'''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004年}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007年}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: * Diameter: approximately 60 meters * Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: * Length: approximately 54 meters * Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. '''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004年}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007年}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: * Diameter: approximately 60 meters * Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: * Length: approximately 54 meters * Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. '''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004年}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007年}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: * Diameter: approximately 60 meters * Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: * Length: approximately 54 meters * Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. '''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004年}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007年}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: * Diameter: approximately 60 meters * Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: * Length: approximately 54 meters * Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. '''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004年}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007年}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: * Diameter: approximately 60 meters * Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: * Length: approximately 54 meters * Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. '''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004年}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007年}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: ** Diameter: approximately 60 meters ** Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: ** Length: approximately 54 meters ** Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. '''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004th year }} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007th year }} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: ** Diameter: approximately 60 meters ** Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: ** Length: approximately 54 meters ** Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. revision.text:
'''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群|12=draft}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方|12=draft}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺|12=draft}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004th year|12=draft}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007th year|12=draft}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳|12=draft}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|12=draft}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: ** Diameter: approximately 60 meters ** Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: ** Length: approximately 54 meters ** Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳|12=draft}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|12=draft}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhol ... 5 KB (646 words) - 05:31, 20 January 2025 List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukushima) | align="center"|[[Tamayama Kofun]]

'''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群|12=draft|12=draft}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004th year|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007th year|12=draft|12=draft}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: ** Diameter: approximately 60 meters ** Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: ** Length: approximately 54 meters ** Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|12=draft|12=draft}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhol ... 5 KB (646 words) - 05:31, 20 January 2025 List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukushima) | align="center"|[[Tamayama Kofun]]

'''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群|12=draft|12=draft}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004th year|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007th year|12=draft|12=draft}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: ** Diameter: approximately 60 meters ** Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: ** Length: approximately 54 meters ** Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|12=draft|12=draft}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhol ... 5 KB (646 words) - 05:31, 20 January 2025 List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukushima) | align="center"|[[Tamayama Kofun]]

revision.text:
{{日本の古墳 |名称 = 玉山古墳 |画像 = {{画像募集中|cat=いわき市}} |別名 = 玉山1号墳/金光寺古墳{{Sfn|玉山古墳群（平凡社）|1993}}{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=1-6}} |所属 = 玉山古墳群 |所在地 = [[福島県]][[いわき市]]四倉町玉山字林崎 |緯度度 = 37|緯度分 = 6|緯度秒 = 53.98 |経度度 =140|経度分 = 56|経度秒 = 37.78 |ISO = JP-07 |形状 = [[前方後円墳]] |規模 = 墳丘長112m
高さ10m（後円部） |築造年代 = [[4世紀]]中葉 |埋葬施設 = 不明 |被葬者 = |出土品 = 土器片 |史跡指定 = 福島県指定史跡「玉山古墳」 |特記事項 = [[東北地方]]第4位/福島県第3位の規模 |地図2 = {{Location map+|Japan Fukushima|width=220|float=center|caption=|places= {{Location map~|Japan Fukushima|lat_deg=37|lat_min=35|lat_sec=34.89|lon_deg=139|lon_min=49|lon_sec=44.77|position=top|mark=前方後円墳-西北西.svg|marksize=24|label=[[亀ヶ森・鎮守森古墳|亀ヶ森古墳]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Fukushima|lat_deg=37|lat_min=30|lat_sec=46.83|lon_deg=139|lon_min=56|lon_sec=38.84|position=bottom|mark=前方後円墳-北北西.svg|marksize=24|label=[[会津大塚山古墳|会津大塚山
古墳]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Fukushima|lat_deg=37|lat_min=6|lat_sec=53.98|lon_deg=140|lon_min=56|lon_sec=37.78|position=left|mark=前方後円墳-西.svg|marksize=24|label='''玉山古墳'''}} {{Location map~|Japan Fukushima|lat_deg=36|lat_min=59|lat_sec=7.56|lon_deg=140|lon_min=53|lon_sec=43.74|position=left|mark=前方後円墳-西南西.svg|marksize=24|label=[[塚前古墳]]}}}} }} '''玉山古墳'''（たまやまこふん、玉山1号墳）は、[[福島県]][[いわき市]]四倉町玉山にある[[古墳]]。形状は[[前方後円墳]]。福島県指定史跡に指定されている。 福島県では第3位、[[東北地方]]では第4位の規模の古墳で東北地方における主な古墳は次の通り。 # [[雷神山古墳]]（宮城県名取市） - 墳丘長168[[メートル]]。 # [[亀ヶ森・鎮守森古墳#亀ヶ森古墳|亀ヶ森古墳]]（福島県河沼郡会津坂下町） - 墳丘長129メートル。 # [[会津大塚山古墳]]（福島県会津若松市） - 墳丘長114メートル。 # '''玉山古墳'''（福島県いわき市） - 墳丘長112メートル。 # [[遠見塚古墳]]（宮城県仙台市） - 墳丘長110メートル。 # 青塚古墳（宮城県大崎市） - 墳丘長100メートル。 # [[稲荷森古墳]]（山形県南陽市） - 墳丘長96メートル。 また近年の調査では、規模全容は未判明ながら次の古墳が大型古墳と推定される。 * [[南森古墳]]（山形県南陽市） - 墳丘長150-168メートル。 * [[塚前古墳]]（福島県いわき市） - 墳丘長95-120メートル。、[[4世紀]]中葉（[[古墳時代]]前期）頃の築造と推定される。 == 概要 == 福島県東部の[[浜通り|浜通り地方]]、仁井田川北岸の支丘陵先端の平坦面に築造された大型前方後円墳である{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=7-18}}。現在は山麓に建立された至宝山金光寺の裏山になり、墳丘南側は墓地利用されて大きく改変を受けている{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=111-130}}。これまでに[[2004年|2004]]-[[2007年]]度（[[平成]]16-19年度）にいわき市教育委員会による発掘調査が実施されている{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=1-6}}。 墳形は前方後円形で、前方部を西方に向ける。墳丘は後円部では3段築成であるが、前方部では旧状不明瞭で段築不明{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=131-135}}。墳丘長は112メートルを測り、福島県では第3位、[[東北地方]]では第4位の規模になる。墳丘外表では[[葺石]]が認められる一方{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=131-135}}、[[埴輪]]は認められず、墳丘周囲では周溝も認められていない{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=131-135}}。埋葬施設は未調査のため明らかではない。出土遺物としては二重口縁壺（底部穿孔壺）等の土器片がある{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=131-135}}。 この玉山古墳は、出土土器より[[古墳時代]]前期後葉-末葉の[[4世紀]]中葉頃の築造と推定される{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=131-135}}。当時は東北地方南部で古墳が大型化する時期とされ、時期差はあるが本古墳と同等規模の[[会津大塚山古墳]]（[[福島県]][[会津若松市]]、114メートル）・[[遠見塚古墳]]（[[宮城県]][[仙台市]]、110メートル）との関連性が指摘される{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=131-135}}。また[[梵天山古墳]]（[[茨城県]][[常陸太田市]]）から本古墳の地域までは大型古墳の空白地であるとともに、本古墳は大型・3段築成・葺石という要素を持つことから、被葬者は[[ヤマト王権]]と結びついて交通の要衝である当地を押さえ、一帯を広域的に支配した様子が示唆される{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=131-135}}。 古墳域は[[1980年]]（[[昭和]]55年）に福島県指定史跡に指定されている。 == 遺跡歴 == * [[宝暦]]年間の『至宝山金光寺縁起』に、金光寺北山に開山上人ゆかりの品の埋葬の旨の記述。墳丘上には宝暦13年（[[1763年]]）銘の供養塔が所在{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=1-6}}。 * [[1940年]]（[[昭和]]15年）、佐久良春彦による紹介{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=1-6}}。 * [[1979年]]（昭和54年）、測量調査{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=1-6}}。 * [[1980年]]（昭和55年）3月28日、福島県指定史跡に指定。 * [[2004年|2004]]-[[2007年]]度（[[平成]]16-19年度）、範囲確認の測量調査・発掘調査（いわき市教育委員会、[[2009年]]（平成21年）に報告書刊行）{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=1-6}}。 == 墳丘 == 墳丘の規模は次の通り（2004-2007年度（平成16-19年度）の調査値）{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=131-135}}。 * 墳丘長：約112メートル * 後円部 - 3段築成。 ** 直径：約60メートル ** 高さ：約10メートル * 前方部 - 段築不明。 ** 長さ：約54メートル ** 高さ：約6メートル 墳丘長に関してはかつて118メートルという数字が知られたが、発掘調査による葺石基底石を押さえた規模としては上記の値に修正される{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=131-135}}。ただし墳丘の造作は葺石の外側にも延びていることから、墳丘は上記の値よりも大きくなる可能性がある{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=131-135}}。また現在は墳丘の南側が墓地化して詳細不明であるほか、北側でも特に前方部で旧状から大きく改変を受けていることが判明している{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=111-130}}。 なお、墳丘裾部では1間×2間の建物状遺構が検出されており、これは[[殯|モガリ]]（殯）儀礼を行う喪屋的施設の可能性があるとして注目される{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=131-135}}。 == 文化財 == === 福島県指定文化財 === * 史跡 ** 玉山古墳 - 1980年（昭和55年）3月28日指定{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.iwaki.lg.jp/www/contents/1001000004791/files/bunkazai.pdf 指定文化財一覧（平成29年5月1日現在）]}}（いわき市ホームページ）。。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''注釈''' {{reflist|group="注"}} '''出典''' {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用した文献）}} * 地方自治体発行 ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=財団法人いわき市教育文化事業団編|author=|year=2009|chapter=|title=[https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/23358 県指定史跡 玉山古墳 -東北南部における前方後円墳の調査-（いわき市埋蔵文化財調査報告 第135冊）]|publisher=いわき市教育委員会|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009}}}} - リンクは奈良文化財研究所「全国遺跡報告総覧」。 * 事典類 ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=川崎純徳|year=1989|chapter=玉山1号墳|title=[[日本古墳大辞典]]|publisher=[[東京堂出版]]|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|玉山1号墳（古墳）|1989}}}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=玉山古墳群|title=[[日本歴史地名大系]] 7 福島県の地名|publisher=[[平凡社]]|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|玉山古墳群（平凡社）|1993}}}} == 関連文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用していない関連文献）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=財団法人いわき市教育文化事業団編|author=|year=2005|chapter=|title=玉山古墳 -平成16年度範囲確認調査概報-|publisher=いわき市教育委員会|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=財団法人いわき市教育文化事業団編|author=|year=2006|chapter=|title=玉山古墳 -平成17年度範囲確認調査概報-|publisher=いわき市教育委員会|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=財団法人いわき市教育文化事業団編|author=|year=2007|chapter=|title=玉山古墳 -平成18年度範囲確認調査概報-|publisher=いわき市教育委員会|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=財団法人いわき市教育文化事業団編|author=|year=2008|chapter=|title=玉山古墳 -平成19年度範囲確認調査概報-|publisher=いわき市教育委員会|isbn=|ref=}} == 関連項目 == * [[塚前古墳]]（いわき市小名浜林城） - 墳丘長95-120メートルの前方後円墳。後期古墳としては東北地方で最大規模。 * [[石城国造]] {{デフォルトソート:たまやまこふん}} [[Category:福島県の古墳]] [[Category:いわき市の歴史]] [[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:福島県指定史跡]]

3段築成。 ** 直径：約60メートル ** 高さ：約10メートル * 前方部 - 段築不明。 ** 長さ：約54メートル ** 高さ：約6メートル 墳丘長に関してはかつて118メートルという数字が知られたが、発掘調査による葺石基底石を押さえた規模としては上記の値に修正される{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=131-135}}。ただし墳丘の造作は葺石の外側にも延びていることから、墳丘は上記の値よりも大きくなる可能性がある{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=131-135}}。また現在は墳丘の南側が墓地化して詳細不明であるほか、北側でも特に前方部で旧状から大きく改変を受けていることが判明している{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=111-130}}。 なお、墳丘裾部では1間×2間の建物状遺構が検出されており、これは[[殯|モガリ]]（殯）儀礼を行う喪屋的施設の可能性があるとして注目される{{Sfn|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009|pp=131-135}}。 == 文化財 == === 福島県指定文化財 === * 史跡 ** 玉山古墳 - 1980年（昭和55年）3月28日指定{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.iwaki.lg.jp/www/contents/1001000004791/files/bunkazai.pdf 指定文化財一覧（平成29年5月1日現在）]}}（いわき市ホームページ）。。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''注釈''' {{reflist|group="注"}} '''出典''' {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用した文献）}} * 地方自治体発行 ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=財団法人いわき市教育文化事業団編|author=|year=2009|chapter=|title=[https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/23358 県指定史跡 玉山古墳 -東北南部における前方後円墳の調査-（いわき市埋蔵文化財調査報告 第135冊）]|publisher=いわき市教育委員会|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|県指定史跡 玉山古墳|2009}}}} - リンクは奈良文化財研究所「全国遺跡報告総覧」。 * 事典類 ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=川崎純徳|year=1989|chapter=玉山1号墳|title=[[日本古墳大辞典]]|publisher=[[東京堂出版]]|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|玉山1号墳（古墳）|1989}}}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=玉山古墳群|title=[[日本歴史地名大系]] 7 福島県の地名|publisher=[[平凡社]]|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|玉山古墳群（平凡社）|1993}}}} == 関連文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用していない関連文献）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=財団法人いわき市教育文化事業団編|author=|year=2005|chapter=|title=玉山古墳 -平成16年度範囲確認調査概報-|publisher=いわき市教育委員会|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=財団法人いわき市教育文化事業団編|author=|year=2006|chapter=|title=玉山古墳 -平成17年度範囲確認調査概報-|publisher=いわき市教育委員会|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=財団法人いわき市教育文化事業団編|author=|year=2007|chapter=|title=玉山古墳 -平成18年度範囲確認調査概報-|publisher=いわき市教育委員会|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=財団法人いわき市教育文化事業団編|author=|year=2008|chapter=|title=玉山古墳 -平成19年度範囲確認調査概報-|publisher=いわき市教育委員会|isbn=|ref=}} == 関連項目 == * [[塚前古墳]]（いわき市小名浜林城） - 墳丘長95-120メートルの前方後円墳。後期古墳としては東北地方で最大規模。 * [[石城国造]] {{デフォルトソート:たまやまこふん}} [[Category:福島県の古墳]] [[Category:いわき市の歴史]] [[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:福島県指定史跡]]

'''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun Ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: ** Diameter: approximately 60 meters ** Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: ** Length: approximately 54 meters ** Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhol ... 5 KB (646 words) - 05:31, 20 January 2025 List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukushima) | align="center"|[[Tamayama Kofun]]

'''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun Ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: ** Diameter: approximately 60 meters ** Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: ** Length: approximately 54 meters ** Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhol ... 5 KB (646 words) - 05:31, 20 January 2025 List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukushima) | align="center"|[[Tamayama Kofun]]

'''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|qq=}} located in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|ja|いわき市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwaki, Fukushima|qq=}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushima Prefecture|qq=}}. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun Group|qq=}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun Ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|ja|東北地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōhoku region|qq=}}. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpō-kōen-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|qq=}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from {{ill|2004|ja|2004年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2004|qq=}} to {{ill|2007|ja|2007年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2007|qq=}} revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiishi|qq=}}, though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsukayama Kofun|qq=}} (114 meters) and {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmizuka Kofun|qq=}} (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: ** Diameter: approximately 60 meters ** Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: ** Length: approximately 54 meters ** Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukamae Kofun|qq=}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun|qq=}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun|qq=}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhol ... 5 KB (646 words) - 05:31, 20 January 2025 List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukushima) | align="center"|[[Tamayama Kofun]]

'''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in [[:en:Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki, Fukushima]], [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun Group|qq=}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun Ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tōhoku region]]. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|qq=}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from [[:en:2004|2004]] to [[:en:2007|2007]] revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with [[:en:Fukiishi|Fukiishi]], though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with [[:en:Ōtsukayama Kofun|Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun]] (114 meters) and [[:en:Tōmizuka Kofun|Tōmizuka Kofun]] (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: ** Diameter: approximately 60 meters ** Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: ** Length: approximately 54 meters ** Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:福島県の古墳
revision.text:
'''Tamayama Kofun''' (玉山古墳, also known as '''Tamayama No.1 Kofun''' or '''Konkoji Kofun''') is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in [[:en:Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki, Fukushima]], [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]. It is part of the {{ill|Tamayama Kofun Group|ja|玉山古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun Group|qq=}} and has been designated as a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. This kofun Ranks as the third largest in Fukushima Prefecture and the fourth largest in the [[:en:Tōhoku region|Tōhoku region]]. It is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. == Overview == Tamayama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tomb ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]) situated on a flat terrace along the northern bank of the Niida River in eastern Fukushima. Today, it is located behind the main hall of {{ill|Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|ja|至宝山金光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihōzan Konkoji Temple|qq=}}, with parts of the mound modified for cemetery use. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from [[:en:2004|2004]] to [[:en:2007|2007]] revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with [[:en:Fukiishi|Fukiishi]], though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with [[:en:Ōtsukayama Kofun|Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun]] (114 meters) and [[:en:Tōmizuka Kofun|Tōmizuka Kofun]] (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: ** Diameter: approximately 60 meters ** Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: ** Length: approximately 54 meters ** Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009). ''Tamayama Kofun: Excavation Report.'' * Kawazaki, Juntoku (1989). ''Japanese Kofun Dictionary.'' Tokyo: Tokyodo Publishing. * Heibonsha (1993). ''History and Topography of Japan: Fukushima Prefecture.'' == Related Sites == * {{ill|Tsukamae Kofun|ja|塚前古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukamae Kofun|qq=}} (Iwaki City) - A later kofun, estimated at 95-120 meters. * {{ill|Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikawa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}} - Regional rulers potentially associated with Tamayama Kofun. # {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun|qq=}} (Iwaki, Fukushima) - 112 meters in length. * {{ill|Tamayama Kofun|ja|玉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamayama Kofun|qq=}} (Yotsukura, Iwaki) - Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. 112-meter keyhol ... 5 KB (646 words) - 05:31, 20 January 2025 List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukushima) | align="center"|[[Tamayama Kofun]]

Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. Archaeological excavations by the Iwaki City Board of Education from [[:en:2004|2004]] to [[:en:2007|2007]] revealed key features of the tumulus: * The mound is built in three tiers in the circular section, while the exact structure of the front section remains uncertain. * Total length: 112 meters (third largest in Fukushima, fourth in Tōhoku). * Outer surface covered with [[:en:Fukiishi|Fukiishi]], though no haniwa have been found. * Burial facility remains unexcavated; excavated artifacts include shards of double-lipped earthenware jars. The tomb's construction is associated with the early expansion of large-scale kofun in southern Tōhoku. Comparisons have been drawn with [[:en:Ōtsukayama Kofun|Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun]] (114 meters) and [[:en:Tōmizuka Kofun|Tōmizuka Kofun]] (110 meters), indicating links to Yamato rulers and regional governance. == Historical Significance == The site has been historically documented in records related to Konkoji Temple and has been surveyed multiple times: * Edo period: Mentioned in temple records of Konkoji. * 1940 (Shōwa 15): First introduced in academic literature. * 1979 (Shōwa 54): Surveyed for size and structure. * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Designated a Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site. * 2004–2007: Excavations confirmed mound structure and burial context. == Mound Dimensions == According to the 2004–2007 excavation survey: * Total length: approximately 112 meters. * Circular rear section: ** Diameter: approximately 60 meters ** Height: approximately 10 meters * Rectangular front section: ** Length: approximately 54 meters ** Height: approximately 6 meters Previous estimates of 118 meters were revised after precise measurements of the foundation stones. Further investigations may reveal additional extensions. A structure near the mound base has been identified as a possible mourning site for funerary rituals. == Cultural Properties == === Fukushima Prefectural Historic Site === * Tamayama Kofun - Designated on March 28, 1980. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Iwaki City Board of Education (2009).
